claim: Compute advantage = (compute_scaling × autonomy) / (time + effort + monetary_cost) is a framework for evaluating AI coding tools
actionable: Apply this equation to your AI coding tool decisions: score each variable 1-100 and compare tools systematically rather than subjectively
source_title: My SIMPLE FRAMEWORK to PICK the BEST AI Coding Tools (Aider vs Claude Code)

claim: Compute scaling can be increased through prompt chains, agentic loops, and multi-step model calls, not just by upgrading the model
actionable: Design your AI coding workflows to use chained prompts and agentic loops before paying for more expensive models
source_title: My SIMPLE FRAMEWORK to PICK the BEST AI Coding Tools (Aider vs Claude Code)

claim: Claude Code increases autonomy significantly compared to Aider because it can write code and call arbitrary tools automatically
actionable: Use Claude Code when you need high autonomy for tool calling; use Aider when you need customizable workflows and don't need automatic tool use
source_title: My SIMPLE FRAMEWORK to PICK the BEST AI Coding Tools (Aider vs Claude Code)

claim: Claude Code's high monetary cost ($70/usage compared to Aider's $10) is the primary trade-off against its higher autonomy and compute scaling
actionable: Calculate whether Claude Code's cost is justified for your use case by measuring time savings; if monthly bill exceeds $300, evaluate cheaper alternatives like Minimax M2.5
source_title: My SIMPLE FRAMEWORK to PICK the BEST AI Coding Tools (Aider vs Claude Code)

claim: Devon achieves the highest compute advantage score (nearly double Claude Code) through extreme autonomy (70+) and minimal time cost (5)
actionable: Use Devon for large, complex projects where you can afford $500/month; the high autonomy makes hands-off iteration possible
source_title: My SIMPLE FRAMEWORK to PICK the BEST AI Coding Tools (Aider vs Claude Code)
